giovanni-pierluigi-da-palestrina.name: Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina
giovanni-pierluigi-da-palestrina.biography: The master of Renaissance polyphony. Palestrina's smooth, flowing counterpoint became the model for sacred music composition for centuries. His masses and motets represent the pinnacle of the Roman School, and his style was later codified as the foundation of counterpoint study.

tomas-luis-de-victoria.name: Tomás Luis de Victoria
tomas-luis-de-victoria.biography: The greatest Spanish composer of the Renaissance and one of the most important polyphonists of his era. Victoria devoted himself exclusively to sacred music, producing masses, motets, and the deeply expressive Officium Defunctorum. His intensely emotional style set him apart from his Roman School contemporaries.

claudio-monteverdi.name: Claudio Monteverdi
claudio-monteverdi.biography: A revolutionary figure who bridged the Renaissance and Baroque. Monteverdi's operas — especially L'Orfeo — established opera as a major art form. His madrigals pushed the boundaries of harmony and text expression, marking the transition from Renaissance polyphony to Baroque drama.

henry-purcell.name: Henry Purcell
henry-purcell.biography: England's greatest Baroque composer. Purcell's remarkably expressive music uniquely blended Italian and French influences with English traditions. His opera Dido and Aeneas contains one of the most famous laments in all music, and his theatre music set a standard unmatched in England until the 20th century.

antonio-vivaldi.name: Antonio Vivaldi
antonio-vivaldi.biography: The 'Red Priest' of Venice, Vivaldi was one of the most prolific and influential Baroque composers. His concertos — especially The Four Seasons — established the three-movement concerto form and influenced generations of composers including Bach. He wrote over 500 concertos and 46 operas.

georg-philipp-telemann.name: Georg Philipp Telemann
georg-philipp-telemann.biography: The most prolific major composer in history, Telemann produced over 3,000 works spanning every genre of his time. As music director of Hamburg's principal churches and the Oper am Gänsemarkt, he was the most famous German composer of his generation, eclipsing even his friend J.S. Bach in contemporary reputation.

jean-philippe-rameau.name: Jean-Philippe Rameau
jean-philippe-rameau.biography: The leading French composer of the 18th century and a pioneering music theorist whose Treatise on Harmony laid the foundations for modern tonal theory. Rameau revolutionized French opera with works of unprecedented harmonic richness and dramatic power, succeeding Lully as the dominant force in French musical theatre.

johann-sebastian-bach.name: Johann Sebastian Bach
johann-sebastian-bach.biography: Often regarded as the greatest composer who ever lived. Bach brought Baroque counterpoint to its supreme height, creating works of unmatched intellectual depth and emotional power. From the monumental Mass in B minor to the intimate Cello Suites, his music encompasses virtually every genre of his era except opera.

george-frideric-handel.name: George Frideric Handel
george-frideric-handel.biography: Born in the same year as Bach, Handel took the opposite path — cosmopolitan, theatrical, and enormously successful in his lifetime. His operas dominated London's theatres, and when fashions changed, he reinvented himself as the father of the English oratorio. Messiah remains one of the most performed choral works in history.

domenico-scarlatti.name: Domenico Scarlatti
domenico-scarlatti.biography: A virtuoso harpsichordist who spent much of his career at the Spanish and Portuguese courts, Scarlatti composed over 550 keyboard sonatas that explored radical new techniques. His single-movement sonatas, with their hand-crossing passages, rapid repetitions, and Iberian folk influences, expanded the expressive and technical possibilities of keyboard music.

christoph-willibald-gluck.name: Christoph Willibald Gluck
christoph-willibald-gluck.biography: The great reformer of opera who stripped away the excesses of Baroque opera seria to create a more natural, dramatically powerful art form. His reform operas, beginning with Orfeo ed Euridice, subordinated vocal display to dramatic truth and influenced every subsequent opera composer from Mozart to Wagner.

joseph-haydn.name: Joseph Haydn
joseph-haydn.biography: The 'Father of the Symphony' and 'Father of the String Quartet.' Haydn's long career at the Esterházy court gave him the freedom to experiment relentlessly, developing the symphony from a lightweight entertainment into a profound artistic statement. His humor, formal ingenuity, and melodic gift made him the most celebrated composer in Europe.

wolfgang-amadeus-mozart.name: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
wolfgang-amadeus-mozart.biography: A prodigy who became a titan. Mozart's music achieves a perfection of form and expression that seems almost superhuman. In his brief 35 years, he mastered every genre — symphony, concerto, opera, chamber music, sacred music — and infused each with a depth of feeling and dramatic power that transcends the Classical era's ideals of balance and restraint.

ludwig-van-beethoven.name: Ludwig van Beethoven
ludwig-van-beethoven.biography: The composer who changed everything. Beethoven took the Classical forms of Haydn and Mozart and shattered their boundaries, pouring Romantic passion into structures of unprecedented scale and dramatic force. His battle with deafness is one of music's greatest tragedies and triumphs — the Ninth Symphony, composed almost entirely deaf, remains humanity's most powerful musical statement of brotherhood.

niccolo-paganini.name: Niccolò Paganini
niccolo-paganini.biography: The most legendary violin virtuoso in history, whose astonishing technique and stage presence spawned rumors of a diabolical pact. Paganini's 24 Caprices for solo violin set a new standard for instrumental virtuosity that inspired Liszt, Schumann, Brahms, and Rachmaninoff to push the boundaries of their own instruments.

carl-maria-von-weber.name: Carl Maria von Weber
carl-maria-von-weber.biography: The father of German Romantic opera, whose Der Freischütz established a new kind of opera rooted in German folklore, supernatural atmosphere, and orchestral colour. Weber's innovations in orchestration and dramatic characterization laid the groundwork for Wagner's music dramas and the entire tradition of German operatic Romanticism.

franz-schubert.name: Franz Schubert
franz-schubert.biography: The supreme melodist. Schubert's gift for song — over 600 Lieder — transformed the art song into a profound vehicle for poetry and emotion. He died at 31, leaving behind an astonishing body of work including the 'Unfinished' Symphony, the 'Great' C major Symphony, and some of the most beautiful chamber music ever written.

hector-berlioz.name: Hector Berlioz
hector-berlioz.biography: The great orchestral innovator. Berlioz revolutionized the symphony with the Symphonie fantastique, one of music's first great programmatic works — a hallucinatory, autobiographical journey that expanded the orchestra's palette in ways no one had imagined. His Treatise on Instrumentation became the bible of orchestration.

felix-mendelssohn.name: Felix Mendelssohn
felix-mendelssohn.biography: A Romantic with a Classical soul. Mendelssohn combined Romantic expressiveness with Classical clarity and elegance. He revived Bach's music, founded the Leipzig Conservatory, and composed works of luminous beauty — from the radiant 'Italian' Symphony to the ethereal Midsummer Night's Dream overture, written at age 17.

frederic-chopin.name: Frédéric Chopin
frederic-chopin.biography: The poet of the piano. Chopin composed almost exclusively for his instrument, but within that focus achieved an unrivaled depth of expression. His nocturnes, études, preludes, and ballades redefined what the piano could say. A Polish exile in Paris, his music is infused with both Slavic melancholy and Parisian elegance.

robert-schumann.name: Robert Schumann
robert-schumann.biography: Schumann was the quintessential Romantic — passionate, literary, torn between opposing impulses he embodied as the characters Florestan and Eusebius. His piano works, songs, and symphonies burn with poetic intensity. As a critic, he championed Chopin, Brahms, and Berlioz, shaping the Romantic movement itself.

franz-liszt.name: Franz Liszt
franz-liszt.biography: The first modern superstar musician. Liszt's virtuosity at the piano was legendary — audiences fainted, riots broke out. But beyond the spectacle, he was a profound innovator: he invented the symphonic poem, developed thematic transformation, and in his late works explored harmonies so radical they anticipated the 20th century.

richard-wagner.name: Richard Wagner
richard-wagner.biography: The most revolutionary and controversial figure in 19th-century music. Wagner's concept of the Gesamtkunstwerk — the 'total work of art' — fused music, drama, poetry, and visual spectacle into the monumental music dramas of the Ring cycle and Tristan und Isolde. His chromatic harmony pushed tonality to its breaking point.

giuseppe-verdi.name: Giuseppe Verdi
giuseppe-verdi.biography: The Shakespearean dramatist of opera. Verdi's operas — Rigoletto, La Traviata, Aida, Otello, Falstaff — combine irresistible melody with devastating psychological insight. He was also a symbol of Italian unification; 'Viva VERDI!' became a political rallying cry (standing for 'Vittorio Emanuele Re D'Italia').

cesar-franck.name: César Franck
cesar-franck.biography: A deeply spiritual organist-composer who became the revered teacher of an entire generation of French musicians. Franck's mature works — composed mostly in the last decade of his life — introduced cyclic form and chromatic harmony that profoundly influenced Debussy, Ravel, and the French symphonic tradition.

bedrich-smetana.name: Bedřich Smetana
bedrich-smetana.biography: The father of Czech music, Smetana forged a national musical identity through works steeped in Bohemian folk traditions and patriotic sentiment. His cycle of symphonic poems Má vlast and the opera The Bartered Bride remain cornerstones of Czech culture, composed even as progressive deafness overtook him.

johannes-brahms.name: Johannes Brahms
johannes-brahms.biography: The great conservative-progressive. Brahms stood against the 'New Music' of Wagner and Liszt, championing Classical forms — but filled them with such harmonic richness, rhythmic complexity, and emotional depth that he expanded them from within. His four symphonies, the German Requiem, and his chamber music are cornerstones of the repertoire.

alexander-borodin.name: Alexander Borodin
alexander-borodin.biography: A distinguished chemist and member of the nationalist group known as The Five, Borodin composed relatively few works, but each one is a masterpiece of melodic richness and exotic colour. His unfinished opera Prince Igor and the orchestral tone poem In the Steppes of Central Asia capture the vast landscapes and oriental influences of the Russian imagination.

camille-saint-saens.name: Camille Saint-Saëns
camille-saint-saens.biography: A child prodigy of Mozartean precocity, Saint-Saëns became the elder statesman of French music through a career spanning over seven decades. His elegant craftsmanship produced enduring works in every genre — from the Organ Symphony and piano concertos to the playful Carnival of the Animals — blending classical form with Romantic colour and French clarity.

modest-mussorgsky.name: Modest Mussorgsky
modest-mussorgsky.biography: The most daringly original of The Five, Mussorgsky rejected Western European academic conventions to forge a raw, distinctly Russian musical language. His opera Boris Godunov and the piano suite Pictures at an Exhibition reveal an uncompromising vision — rugged, harmonically bold, and deeply rooted in the rhythms of Russian speech and folk life.

pyotr-ilyich-tchaikovsky.name: Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky
pyotr-ilyich-tchaikovsky.biography: Russia's most beloved composer. Tchaikovsky's music pulses with intense, often tortured emotion — the desperate passion of Romeo and Juliet, the grandeur of the 1812 Overture, the fairytale enchantment of Swan Lake and The Nutcracker. His six symphonies chart a deeply personal journey from youthful energy to the shattering despair of the Pathétique.

antonin-dvorak.name: Antonín Dvořák
antonin-dvorak.biography: The voice of Bohemia. Dvořák brought Czech folk music into the symphonic mainstream with irresistible warmth and color. His 'New World' Symphony, composed during his time in America, is one of the most popular symphonies ever written. His cello concerto prompted Brahms to exclaim, 'Why didn't I know one could write a cello concerto like this?'

edvard-grieg.name: Edvard Grieg
edvard-grieg.biography: Norway's greatest composer. Grieg captured the landscapes, folk tales, and spirit of Scandinavia in music of extraordinary color and charm. His Piano Concerto and Peer Gynt suites are among the most popular works in the Romantic repertoire, and his Lyric Pieces for piano are miniature gems of Nordic poetry.

nikolai-rimsky-korsakov.name: Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov
nikolai-rimsky-korsakov.biography: The supreme orchestrator of the 19th century and the guiding figure of the Russian nationalist school. As a member of The Five and influential teacher of Stravinsky and Prokofiev, Rimsky-Korsakov shaped the course of Russian music through his glittering orchestral fairy-tale operas and the famous orchestration treatise that remains a standard reference.

edward-elgar.name: Edward Elgar
edward-elgar.biography: The composer who single-handedly ended England's two-century drought of major orchestral music. Elgar's Enigma Variations and two symphonies brought English music into the European mainstream, while his Cello Concerto — a valediction written after the devastation of World War I — stands among the most poignant works of the 20th century.

giacomo-puccini.name: Giacomo Puccini
giacomo-puccini.biography: The last great master of Italian opera, whose gift for soaring melody and theatrical instinct produced some of the most beloved works in the repertoire. La bohème, Tosca, Madama Butterfly, and the unfinished Turandot combine verismo realism with lush orchestration and an unerring sense of dramatic pacing.

gustav-mahler.name: Gustav Mahler
gustav-mahler.biography: The last great Romantic symphonist — and the first voice of modernity. Mahler's ten symphonies are vast, cosmos-spanning works that encompass everything: folk song and funeral march, birdsong and apocalypse, tender intimacy and shattering climax. 'A symphony must be like the world,' he said. 'It must contain everything.'

claude-debussy.name: Claude Debussy
claude-debussy.biography: The founder of musical Impressionism — though he hated the term. Debussy dissolved traditional harmony into shimmering washes of color, creating a new musical language inspired by Symbolist poetry, Japanese art, and the sounds of nature. Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune is often called the beginning of modern music.

richard-strauss.name: Richard Strauss
richard-strauss.biography: Master of the orchestral tone poem and opera. Strauss's dazzling orchestration and dramatic instinct produced works of staggering virtuosity — Also sprach Zarathustra, Der Rosenkavalier, the Four Last Songs. He lived long enough to span the entire Modern era, outliving many who had declared Romanticism dead.

jean-sibelius.name: Jean Sibelius
jean-sibelius.biography: Finland's national composer and one of the last great symphonists, Sibelius drew inspiration from Finnish mythology and the austere Nordic landscape. His seven symphonies trace a remarkable journey from late-Romantic grandeur to an almost mystical compression of form, while Finlandia became an unofficial anthem of Finnish independence.

erik-satie.name: Erik Satie
erik-satie.biography: An eccentric visionary who anticipated minimalism, ambient music, and the avant-garde decades before they emerged. Satie's deceptively simple piano pieces — the Gymnopédies, Gnossiennes, and provocatively titled Vexations — challenged every convention of Romantic excess, profoundly influencing Debussy, Ravel, Cage, and the entire trajectory of 20th-century music.

sergei-rachmaninoff.name: Sergei Rachmaninoff
sergei-rachmaninoff.biography: The last great Romantic pianist-composer. Rachmaninoff's music is defined by sweeping melody, lush harmony, and the most demanding piano writing in the repertoire — all played by the composer himself with legendary virtuosity. His Piano Concerto No. 2 and No. 3 remain audience favorites worldwide.

arnold-schoenberg.name: Arnold Schoenberg
arnold-schoenberg.biography: The most radical innovator in Western music history. Schoenberg's journey from lush post-Romanticism through 'free atonality' to the systematic twelve-tone method reshaped the foundations of musical composition. His work divided the musical world, but his influence — through students Berg and Webern — is incalculable.

maurice-ravel.name: Maurice Ravel
maurice-ravel.biography: The Swiss watchmaker of music — every note precisely placed. Ravel's orchestration is the most brilliant in all of music (his orchestration of Mussorgsky's Pictures at an Exhibition is more famous than the original). Bolero's hypnotic crescendo, Daphnis et Chloé's dawn scene, and his two piano concertos are miracles of color and craft.

bela-bartok.name: Béla Bartók
bela-bartok.biography: Bartók fused the raw energy of Hungarian, Romanian, and Slovak folk music with modernist techniques, creating a unique and powerful musical language. His six string quartets are considered the most important since Beethoven's. The Concerto for Orchestra and Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta are pillars of the 20th-century repertoire.

igor-stravinsky.name: Igor Stravinsky
igor-stravinsky.biography: The chameleon of modern music. Stravinsky's The Rite of Spring caused a riot at its 1913 premiere and changed music forever with its savage rhythms and polytonal clashes. He then reinvented himself as a neoclassicist, then a serialist — each phase producing masterpieces. He remains the most influential composer of the 20th century.

sergei-prokofiev.name: Sergei Prokofiev
sergei-prokofiev.biography: A modernist with an irrepressible melodic gift. Prokofiev's music bristles with motor rhythms, acerbic wit, and unexpected lyricism. From the fairy-tale Peter and the Wolf to the cinematic Romeo and Juliet ballet, from the satirical Classical Symphony to the war-era Piano Sonatas, his range and vitality are extraordinary.

paul-hindemith.name: Paul Hindemith
paul-hindemith.biography: A versatile composer, virtuoso violist, and influential theorist who became one of the leading figures of 20th-century neoclassicism. Hindemith's Gebrauchsmusik philosophy championed practical, accessible composition, while works like Mathis der Maler and his comprehensive treatise The Craft of Musical Composition shaped modern music education.

aaron-copland.name: Aaron Copland
aaron-copland.biography: The dean of American composers, Copland forged a distinctly American sound from jazz rhythms, folk melodies, and wide-open harmonies that evoke the vastness of the American landscape. His ballets Appalachian Spring, Billy the Kid, and Rodeo defined musical Americana, while his teaching and advocacy shaped generations of American composers.

dmitri-shostakovich.name: Dmitri Shostakovich
dmitri-shostakovich.biography: The voice of Soviet suffering. Shostakovich lived under constant threat from Stalin's regime, and his 15 symphonies and 15 string quartets encode a lifetime of fear, defiance, dark humor, and sorrow. His Fifth Symphony — described as 'a Soviet artist's creative reply to just criticism' — is one of the most debated works in music: triumph or irony?

benjamin-britten.name: Benjamin Britten
benjamin-britten.biography: The greatest English opera composer since Purcell, Britten revitalized English music with works of extraordinary dramatic power and technical mastery. From Peter Grimes to The Turn of the Screw and the War Requiem, his output combined accessibility with modernist sophistication, establishing Aldeburgh as a major centre of musical life.
